text:
KINGS NEUROSCIENCES CENTRE


Clinic Date: 02 October 2025 

Dr Wang
Saffron Park Hospital

Saffron Park, London, E14 7JL
Dear Dr Wang
Alex Carter, DOB: 14-05-2006, Hospital No: P903772 NHS No. 7712390045
Flat 3, 12 Kingsbridge Lane, London, E14 6PA 

I reviewed the above patient in the Adult Neurology Clinic today following transition from paediatric services. A parent attended and contributed helpful collateral history. He has a confirmed diagnosis of generalised epilepsy.

This is our first adult clinic review post-transition. From the joint handover summary and today’s history, his typical episodes comprise brief staring spells with eyelid flutter and occasional myoclonic jerks shortly after waking. Generalised tonic–clonic seizures have been infrequent. At present he reports clusters of brief absence episodes every 4 weeks, usually over 1–2 days, often precipitated by reduced sleep and academic stress. His last generalised tonic–clonic seizure was in May 2025 after a night of sleep deprivation, and there have been none since.

He is taking Sodium Valproate 500 mg b.d. and Levetiracetam (Keppra) 1 g b.d. with good adherence. He describes mild morning lethargy and intermittent low mood around examination periods, which he and his parent feel are manageable. No rashes, visual disturbance, or focal neurological symptoms were reported. There is no history of focal onset phenomena. He is not driving.

On examination today he was alert and oriented with normal speech and language. Cranial nerves were intact, tone and power were normal in all limbs, reflexes were symmetric, and coordination and gait were normal. There were no neurocutaneous stigmata.

Paediatric EEGs have shown generalised spike–wave discharges, most recently in 2024. MRI brain (2023) was normal. We will arrange an updated EEG to reassess background and interictal activity in the adult service and to support ongoing management planning. Given his current pattern of absence clusters every 4 weeks without recent convulsions, I have not altered medication today. We discussed adherence, regular sleep, and stress management as practical measures that may help reduce breakthrough events. He and his parent were given information on our transition nurse specialist and epilepsy advisor should they wish to discuss education or lifestyle issues further.

I have asked him to keep a simple seizure diary, noting any absence clusters and possible triggers, particularly sleep loss. We will review him in 6 months, or sooner if events increase in frequency, if he experiences any adverse effects, or if a generalised tonic–clonic seizure recurs.

Yours sincerely

entities.text: clusters of brief absence episodes every 4 weeks, usually over 1–2 days

check__Seizure Frequency Number.analysis: The letter describes current events: "clusters of brief absence episodes every 4 weeks, usually over 1–2 days" and notes no recent generalized tonic–clonic seizures since May 2025. The most explicit numeric frequency given is the clustering pattern: clusters occur every 4 weeks. The number of episodes per cluster is not quantified, only that they are brief absence episodes over 1–2 days. According to the multiple-frequency rule, we choose the highest frequency; however, only the cluster frequency is quantifiable. Therefore, the appropriate normalized label is a cluster-based frequency with unknown episodes per cluster.
seizure_frequency_number: clusters of brief absence episodes every 4 weeks, usually over 1–2 days
